q: When two input streams each have a watermark in a stream-stream join, how does Spark compute the single global watermark and what happens when one stream falls behind?
a: Spark individually tracks the maximum observed event time for each input stream and applies each stream's declared delay to get a per-stream watermark. It then computes a single global watermark using the MINIMUM of the two per-stream watermarks by default. This global watermark is what drives state eviction and, for outer joins, controls when NULL rows are emitted. The min policy is deliberately conservative: if stream B has stalled (upstream outage, partition lag), its watermark stops advancing. Even if stream A's events are hours ahead, the global watermark is pinned at stream B's last-seen event minus B's delay — no state is cleaned up and no outer NULLs are emitted until B recovers. The trade-off is safety over latency: the min policy guarantees you never drop data from the slow stream, but your end-to-end latency is bounded by the slowest producer. You can override this by setting spark.sql.streaming.multipleWatermarkPolicy to max — the global watermark then tracks the fastest stream and state is cleaned up aggressively, but any rows from the slow stream that arrive after the watermark has advanced are silently dropped. In production, always monitor both streams' watermark lag via StreamingQueryProgress.eventTime; a widening gap between the two watermarks is an early warning that your state store is growing and latency is climbing.
fj: Each stream's watermark is independent, so a slow stream doesn't affect how fast the other stream cleans up state.
fs: Watermarks are not independent in a join. Spark merges them into a single global watermark using the min (by default). A slow stream pins the global watermark for ALL stateful operations in the query, blocking state cleanup and outer-join NULL emission across the board.
